key_insights.insight: Good liars ask questions to gather information and avoid disclosing too much about themselves.
key_insights.quote: Good liars ask a lot of questions. Because if you ask questions, you're not really disclosing anything about yourself.

key_insights.insight: Skilled liars align their body language with their words, while unskilled liars often have a disconnect between their body and verbal communication.
key_insights.quote: A skilled liar knows how to appear like they are telling the truth with their words and with their body. Whereas an unskilled liar often has a disconnect and their body will say a different message than what their mouth is saying.

key_insights.insight: Mirroring someone's behavior creates a foundation of trust, but subtly shifting to having them mirror you can establish control in a conversation.
key_insights.quote: So what you need to do is you need to mirror just enough to get to the place where you can get them to mirror you. When they mirror you, subconsciously they know that you're in control.

key_insights.insight: You can't reliably detect lies based on eye movements or microexpressions without establishing a baseline of normal behavior for the individual.
key_insights.quote: The truth is you don't know if someone is lying to you until you've had enough time with the person to establish what's known as a baseline.

key_insights.insight: Core motivations drive people's actions, and understanding these motivations (RICE: Reward, Ideology, Coercion, Ego) allows for more effective influence.
key_insights.quote: If you understand why other people do what they do, all you have to do is connect what they care about with what you want them to do and you just increase the probability of them doing what you want them to do.

key_insights.insight: Ideology is the strongest motivator, followed by ego, reward, and coercion.
key_insights.quote: Ideology is the strongest. Ego is the second strongest. Reward is the third strongest and coercion is the weakest.

key_insights.insight: People operate in three lives: Public, Private, and Secret. Gaining access to someone's 'secret life' builds unbreakable trust.
key_insights.quote: What we're trained to do is to follow a process that allows us to meet somebody in their public life, get them to let us into their private life, and then get them to let us into their secret life.

key_insights.insight: Presenting 'windows' of vulnerability, rather than 'doors', allows for exploration without forcing a change in subject.
key_insights.quote: When it comes to vulnerability and conversing with somebody about vulnerability, you want to present windows and not present doors.

key_insights.insight: Using the 'two and one combination' (two follow-up questions, one confirmation) helps to build rapport and encourage someone to share more information without feeling interrogated.
key_insights.quote: So two means two questions, and one means one confirmation...That way it doesn't feel like you're being interrogated. Instead, it feels like you're talking to somebody who gets you.

actionable_advice.advice: Establish a baseline of normal behavior before assessing honesty.
actionable_advice.how_to: Spend time observing someone's typical demeanor, communication style, and body language in various situations before trying to detect deception.

actionable_advice.advice: Mirror someone's body language to build trust, then subtly lead them to mirror you.
actionable_advice.how_to: Start by subtly mimicking their posture, gestures, and tone. Once rapport is established, gradually shift your own behavior and observe if they unconsciously follow suit.

actionable_advice.advice: Identify a person's core motivations using the RICE framework (Reward, Ideology, Coercion, Ego).
actionable_advice.how_to: Listen carefully to their values, beliefs, and desires. Observe what they respond to positively or negatively. Use active marketing by crafting emotionally laden message to see what people respond to.

actionable_advice.advice: In conversation, use the 'two and one' technique to build rapport and encourage disclosure.
actionable_advice.how_to: When someone shares something, ask two follow-up questions related to their statement. Then, make a confirming statement that shows you understand and empathize with their perspective.

memorable_stories: Description of how the speaker, as a former CIA agent, was taught how to detect liars and elicit information.
Anecdote of how narratives are used in both politics and business.
Example of how asking follow-up questions will make people think that you really care about them.
contrarian_claims: Eye movements and microexpressions are not reliable indicators of lying without an established baseline.
Coercion is the weakest motivator, contrary to how it's often portrayed in movies.
Narrative is not the power in influence, it's actually messaging that is.
episode_title: The Diary Of A CEO with Steven Bartlett - Moment 207: CIA Spy Reveals How To Read Anyone Like A Book!
